source_texts: Circa instans (Nenuphar); Pseudo-Apuleius ch.68 (Nimfa); Dioscorides Latin (Nymphaea)
notes: Nenuphar from Arabic/Persian "ninufar"; standard Circa instans heading. Pseudo-Apuleius uses "Nimfa". Folio f2v and f49r candidates in VMS.

source_texts: Pseudo-Apuleius ch.1 (Plantago); Macer Floridus ch.6 (Plantago); Circa instans (Arnoglossa); Dioscorides (Arnoglossa = lamb's tongue)
notes: Plantago is the FIRST chapter in Pseudo-Apuleius. "Arnoglossa" (= lamb's tongue) is the Circa instans / Dioscorides synonym. Folio f95r candidate in VMS.

medieval_latin_name(s): Elleborus; Elleborus niger; Elleborus albus; Veratrum
source_texts: Dioscorides Latin (Elleborus niger, Elleborus albus); Circa instans (Elleborus)
notes: Medieval texts distinguished "elleborus niger" (true Helleborus) from "elleborus albus" (actually Veratrum album, white hellebore). Anglo-Latin form "elleborus". Folios f3v, f96r candidates in VMS.

source_texts: Macer Floridus (Viola, in Spuria); Circa instans (Viola); Dioscorides Latin (Viola)
notes: Name unchanged from classical Latin through medieval period. Folio f9v (high confidence) in VMS.

source_texts: Pseudo-Apuleius ch.102 (Salvia); Macer Floridus ch.24 (Salvia); Circa instans (Salvia); Dioscorides (Elelisphakon > Salvia in Latin)
notes: Name unchanged. One of the most important medieval medicinal herbs. "Cur moriatur homo cui Salvia crescit in horto?" (Why should a man die while sage grows in his garden?) -- Schola Salernitana. Folio f51v candidate in VMS.

medieval_latin_name(s): Artemisia; Artemisia monoclonos; Artemisia tagantes; Artemisia leptofillos; Absinthium
source_texts: Pseudo-Apuleius ch.10-12 (three Artemisia species); Macer Floridus ch.1 (Artemisia), ch.3 (Absinthium); Circa instans (Artemisia); Dioscorides
notes: FIRST chapter in Macer Floridus. Pseudo-Apuleius has three chapters: monoclonos, tagantes, leptofillos. Absinthium (wormwood) treated separately from Artemisia (mugwort) in most texts.

source_texts: Hildegard of Bingen (Calendula); various medieval herbals (Solsequium = sun-follower)
notes: Solsequium (= following the sun) is the common medieval Latin name, also applied to chicory/heliotrope. "Calendula" from "calendae" (first day of month) referring to long blooming. "Sponsa solis" (bride of the sun) also used. Not in Pseudo-Apuleius or Macer Floridus core text. Folio f12v candidate in VMS.

medieval_latin_name(s): Centauria minor; Centaurium minus; Fel terrae
source_texts: Pseudo-Apuleius ch.35 (Centauria minor); Circa instans (Centaurea); Macer Floridus (Centauria, in Spuria); Dioscorides (Centaurion mikron)
notes: Centauria minor = lesser centaury (Centaurium erythraea). "Centauria maior" = greater centaury (Centaurea centaurium / Rhaponticum). "Fel terrae" (= gall of the earth) is a medieval synonym referring to its extreme bitterness. Both in Pseudo-Apuleius ch.34-35.

source_texts: Pseudo-Apuleius ch.53 (Papaver silvaticum); Dioscorides Latin (Papaver, Mecon); Circa instans (Opium, Papaver)
notes: Pseudo-Apuleius has "Papaver silvaticum" (wild poppy). Circa instans has separate entries for Opium and Papaver. Dioscorides uses "Mecon" (Greek) alongside "Papaver" (Latin). Folio f90r candidate in VMS.

source_texts: Pseudo-Apuleius ch.115 (Cannabis silvatica); Dioscorides Latin (Cannabis); Circa instans (Cannabis)
notes: Pseudo-Apuleius uses "Cannabis silvatica" (wild hemp). Name largely unchanged. Folio f16r candidate in VMS.

source_texts: Dioscorides Latin (Mandragora, with male/female distinction); Circa instans (Mandragora); Pseudo-Apuleius (not in standard text; added in some MSS)
notes: Distinguished as "mas" (male, = M. officinarum) and "femina" (female, = M. autumnalis) following Dioscorides. Among the most famous medieval medicinal plants. Folio f44r candidate in VMS.

source_texts: Circa instans (Borago); Tractatus de Herbis (Borago)
notes: Borago is the standard medieval Latin form. Often confused with or used alongside "Buglossum" (= Anchusa/bugloss, literally "ox-tongue"). Not in Pseudo-Apuleius or Macer Floridus (post-dates these texts; borage entered European cultivation via Arabs ~10th-11th c.). Folio f23v candidate in VMS.

source_texts: Circa instans (Nigella); Dioscorides Latin (Melanthion > Melanthium); various medieval herbals (Git/Gith)
notes: Git or "Gith" is the most common medieval Latin name, from Greek via Arabic. "Melanthium" from Dioscorides. "Nigella" (= little black one) used increasingly in later medieval period. Folio f29v candidate in VMS (Bax decoded text as "kaur" = black).

source_texts: Pseudo-Apuleius ch.128 (Petroselinum); Macer Floridus ch.8 (Apium); Circa instans (Petroselinum)
notes: Petroselinum (= rock celery) is the standard name. Often confused with "Apium" (celery/parsley family). Macer Floridus ch.8 "Apium" covers parsley/celery as a group.

source_texts: Pseudo-Apuleius ch.125 (Feniculum); Macer Floridus ch.17 (Feniculum); Circa instans (Feniculum); Dioscorides (Marathron > Feniculum)
notes: Spelled "Feniculum" in medieval texts (simplified from classical "Foeniculum"). One of the most common medieval herbs for digestion and eyes.

source_texts: Pseudo-Apuleius ch.9 (Botracion statice); ch.8 (Scelerata); Dioscorides Latin (Ranunculus, Batrachion)
notes: Botracion (from Greek Batrachion = frog-plant) in Pseudo-Apuleius. "Scelerata" (= the wicked one) used for the caustic Ranunculus sceleratus. "Apium risus" (= laughing celery) is a medieval synonym.

source_texts: Dioscorides Latin (Aconitum); various medieval herbals (Napellus)
notes: Napellus (= little turnip, from root shape) is the primary medieval Latin name. "Luparia" (= wolf-killer) used in some texts. Not in Pseudo-Apuleius or Macer Floridus core text. Recognized as extremely toxic. Folio f3v candidate in VMS.

source_texts: Not in classical sources
notes: Datura was likely unknown in medieval Europe before 15th-16th century introduction. Some scholars argue late medieval Arabic texts knew it as "jouz mathil" (= nut of Methel). The Latin "Stramonium" appears only in early modern period. If present in VMS, it would suggest late dating or Eastern contact.

source_texts: N/A
notes: New World plant. Cannot have a medieval Latin name. First European descriptions after 1510. Folio f93r proposed as sunflower (O'Neill 1944) but disputed. If correct, implies post-Columbian dating or independent depiction.

source_texts: N/A
notes: New World plant. No medieval Latin name. European awareness only after Columbus. Folio f100r proposed (O'Neill 1944) but "no longer generally accepted."

source_texts: Dioscorides Latin (Cyperus); Circa instans (Galanga); various medieval herbals
notes: Cyperus used for the genus. "Galanga" or "Galingale" specifically for Cyperus longus and Alpinia galanga (true galangal, a different plant). Medieval herbals sometimes conflated aromatic Cyperus with true galangal.

source_texts: Pseudo-Apuleius ch.89 (Millefoium); Dioscorides Latin (Achillea, Millefolium)
notes: Millefolium (= thousand-leaf) is THE standard medieval name. "Herba militaris" (= soldier's herb) used for wound-stanching properties. Pseudo-Apuleius ch.89 specifically. Old English "gearwe."

source_texts: Dioscorides Latin (Ricinus, Cici, Croton); Pseudo-Apuleius (not in standard text)
notes: Ricinus (= tick, from seed shape) is the classical/medieval name. "Cici" and "Croton" are Dioscorides synonyms. Not in Pseudo-Apuleius or Macer Floridus. Folios f6v, f9r candidates in VMS.

source_texts: Pseudo-Apuleius ch.109 (Titimallus), ch.112 (Latirida); Dioscorides Latin (Tithymalus, multiple species)
notes: Tithymalus (from Greek) is the main Dioscorides/medieval name. "Titimallus" is the Pseudo-Apuleius spelling. "Esula" for Euphorbia esula specifically. "Latirida" / "Lathyris" for E. lathyris (caper spurge). Folio f38v candidate in VMS.

source_texts: Pseudo-Apuleius ch.72 (Verbascum); Dioscorides Latin (Verbascum, Phlomos)
notes: Verbascum is the standard classical/medieval name. "Tapsus barbatus" (= bearded tapsus) is a common medieval synonym. "Candela regia" (= royal candle) refers to the tall flowering spike used as a torch.
